text:
How does ADUO work with the PU allocations? Can you only add the upgrades to unused elements?

What I'm thinking is, for those getting ADUO upgrades, will they ultimately have to take a penalty to use upgraded elements for the rest of the season, or end up having to use a mix of old spec and new spec?
label: Analysis
notes: Contains explanation, evidence, or technical reasoning.

text:
Can you only add the upgrades to unused elements?

A lot of the PU is sealed, and any changes to sealed bits of the PU can only be made to new components. Regulated in Appendix C5, 3.10 b:

Any permitted modifications concerning parts that are inside the sealed perimeter (i.e. parts described as “INC” in the “Sealed Perimeter” column of Appendix C4) can only be applied to new Power Unit elements.

Parts that are not sealed seem to mostly be sensors and electrical components; it's listed in detail in Appendix C4. This restriction applies to all upgrades (ADUO as well as reliability), and it means any upgrade will likely have to be introduced through a new component; sealed parts of old components in the pool cannot be upgraded.

Furthermore, any new PU component introduced during a competition must always be of the newest specification, and whenever a new specification is introduced for the first time on any car, all teams supplied by the manufacturer are entitled to priority access to at least 1 PU of that newest specification for the Friday of that competition.
label: Analysis
notes: Contains explanation, evidence, or technical reasoning.

text:
I still remember the Michael Douglas-narrated documentary (on Amazon I believe) that followed McLaren during the 2017 pre-season. Everything looked so wonky (and they clearly painted Honda in a bad light). Hilariously, the documentary ends just before the 2017 season starts, then they fast forward a full year to 2018 with Zak getting the top job and explaining how they dropped Honda and switched to Renault. I read somewhere that they didn't show the actual season because they hadn't gotten F1's clearance to film races (this was all before Drive to Survive), but I couldn't help but think they had decided to cut the losses on what should have been a comeback story documentary over the 2017 season.

As a fan, it felt so bad seeing McLaren struggle so badly in 2015 in their first season with Honda (especially after dropping the best engine on the grid), then get somewhat better in 2016, just to be terrible again in 2017. Those were really dark times. Kudos to Zak, Andrea and the team indeed for such a massive turnaround -- ironically with Mercedes back as their engine provider!
label: Analysis
notes: Contains explanation, evidence, or technical reasoning.

text:
How does ADUO work with the PU allocations? Can you only add the upgrades to unused elements?

What I'm thinking is, for those getting ADUO upgrades, will they ultimately have to take a penalty to use upgraded elements for the rest of the season, or end up having to use a mix of old spec and new spec?
label: Analysis
notes: Contains explanation, evidence, or technical reasoning.

text:
Parts that are not sealed seem to mostly be sensors and electrical components; it's listed in detail in Appendix C4. This restriction applies to all upgrades (ADUO as well as reliability), and it means any upgrade will likely have to be introduced through a new component; sealed parts of old components in the pool cannot be upgraded.

Furthermore, any new PU component introduced during a competition must always be of the newest specification, and whenever a new specification is introduced for the first time on any car, all teams supplied by the manufacturer are entitled to priority access to at least 1 PU of that newest specification for the Friday of that competition.
label: Analysis
notes: Contains explanation, evidence, or technical reasoning.

text:
Whenever a manufacturer introduces a new PU spec, they must grant priority access to 1 PU of that spec to every team they supply. That means, assuming only the regulatory minimum is done, Haas and Cadillac have the guaranteed option to fit the new spec on one of their cars at the same time Ferrari introduces it. However, that regulatory guarantee is limited to one PU and to the first day of the relevant competition.

We don't know what the supply contracts may say beyond that. What can also play into it is that any new element introduced on any car has to always be of the newest specification.

notes: Prediction or strong opinion with limited evidence.
